area: National IHR Focal Point / National IHR Authority
current_ihr2005_anchoring_pattern: Relatively stronger statutory-administrative anchoring. Art. 35 frac. XIX of the Reglamento Interior SS 2025 (MEX-017) explicitly designates DGE as the RSI National Focal Point. NOM-017 numeral 6.6.1-6.6.2 reinforces this at regulatory level. Actor fit is strong; operational procedures for 24-hour availability, WHO communication protocols, and backup coverage are not fully codified in the available corpus. Anchoring level: 3/5 (OBL-001).
main_mexico_actors: Dirección General de Epidemiología (DGE); Secretaría de Salud (SS); DGRI-SS; SRE
ihr2024_implication: IHR 2024 amendments (Arts. 1 and 4) introduce a distinct National IHR Authority concept, separate from the IHR National Focal Point. States Parties are required to designate or establish a National IHR Authority and notify WHO of its contact details. This may require review of whether the DGE NFP designation is sufficient or whether a broader multi-sector National IHR Authority with inter-ministerial coordination functions needs to be formally designated. IHR24-CHG-001, 006, 007, 008, 009.
pandemic_agreement_implication: Relevant to national governance: PA Art. 15(2) urges each Party to establish or strengthen a national pandemic coordination mechanism with whole-of-government and whole-of-society approaches. The National IHR Authority and pandemic coordination functions are likely to overlap or need alignment. PA_ART15_002.
capacity_building_entry_point: Verify legal basis and operational protocols of DGE as IHR National Focal Point; clarify whether the 2024 National IHR Authority requirement calls for a broader institutional designation or can be fulfilled by strengthening existing DGE functions; map inter-ministerial coordination mechanisms needed for full National IHR Authority functions.
notes: Based on IHR 2005 mapping (OBL-001), IHR24-CHG-001/006-009, PA_ART15_002. The distinction between National IHR Authority and IHR National Focal Point is new in the 2024 amendments and has not been assessed against Mexican institutional law in this mapping. Expert constitutional/administrative law review required.

current_ihr2005_anchoring_pattern: Partial statutory anchoring. LGS Art. 134 establishes SINAVE with a national surveillance mandate. NOM-017-SSA2-2012 provides the principal regulatory framework. DGE operational authority is anchored in Reglamento Interior SS Art. 35. Self-assessment obligation (IHR Annex 1A par. 2) has no domestic anchoring identified. Annex 1A benchmarks (community, intermediate, national levels) are partially met by SINAVE governance but not with IHR-specific minimum capacity specifications. Anchoring levels: 0 (OBL-011), 2 (OBL-002, 012, 013, 014, 015).
main_mexico_actors: DGE; SS; CONAVE; CENAPRECE; entidades federativas (SINAVE nodes); COFEPRIS
main_gap_type: regulatory gap; procedural gap; federal implementation gap
ihr2024_implication: IHR 2024 Annex 1 amendments significantly expand the minimum capacity requirements: local-level capacities now include health services access and community engagement; intermediate-level capacities add coordination, risk communication, and misinformation/disinformation management; national-level capacities add clinical guidance, health product access, and risk communication. Prevention is added to the IHR purpose (Art. 2) and to core capacity obligations (Art. 5, Annex 1 title). IHR24-CHG-004, 010, 047, 049, 050, 051. May require review of SINAVE scope and capacity benchmarks.
pandemic_agreement_implication: Relevant to prevention, preparedness, and surveillance: PA Arts. 4 and 5 require multisectoral surveillance for early detection, community-level reporting mechanisms, and a One Health approach. PA_ART04_005, PA_ART04_006, PA_ART05_001.
capacity_building_entry_point: Review whether SINAVE legal framework and NOM-017 are adequate to anchor expanded IHR 2024 Annex 1 core capacity requirements; assess whether community-level and intermediate-level surveillance capacities are operationally specified in state-level instruments; verify whether the IHR self-assessment mechanism (Annex 1A par. 2) has a domestic procedural equivalent.
notes: Based on OBL-002/003/011-015 in IHR 2005 mapping; IHR24-CHG-004/010/047-051; PA_ART04_005-006/PA_ART05_001-003. State-level SINAVE implementation not covered by this corpus. PABS dependency is partial because pathogen access and sequence sharing (PABS draft) connects to laboratory surveillance capacity.

current_ihr2005_anchoring_pattern: Relatively stronger statutory-administrative anchoring for the primary notification obligation. LGS Art. 136 frac. I provides the general notification mandate; Reglamento LGS Sanidad Internacional (MEX-016) Art. 14 assigns a specific duty to notify WHO. DGE NFP designation provides the institutional channel. The Annex 2 decision instrument is not domestically codified. Post-notification continuous reporting (IHR Art. 6(2)) has partial anchoring only. Anchoring levels: 3 (OBL-004), 2 (OBL-005, 006), 1 (OBL-007, 008, 009).
ihr2024_implication: IHR 2024 Art. 8 strengthens the consultation obligation from optional to expected ('may' to 'should'), potentially increasing the procedural requirements for non-notification event consultation with WHO. IHR24-CHG-012. Annex 2 decision instrument also updated to include clusters of severe acute respiratory disease. IHR24-CHG-053. These may require review of notification procedures and DGE/DGRI operational protocols.
pandemic_agreement_implication: Relevant to information and reporting: PA Art. 6(3) requires Parties to develop and maintain national surveillance and reporting systems. PA Art. 21 establishes periodic reporting obligations to the Conference of the Parties. PA_ART06_003, PA_ART21_001, PA_ART21_002.
capacity_building_entry_point: Verify whether DGE has documented internal procedures for Annex 2 risk assessment; review whether the strengthened Art. 8 consultation expectation requires formalisation of WHO consultation protocols at DGE or DGRI level; assess whether Annex 2 decision instrument updates (clusters of severe acute respiratory disease) are reflected in SINAVE alert criteria.
notes: Based on OBL-004/005/006/007/008/009. IHR24-CHG-011/012/053. The notification framework has the strongest IHR 2005 anchoring in the Mexican corpus, but operational gaps (time windows, WHO formats) are well-documented.

current_ihr2005_anchoring_pattern: Weak anchoring. IHR obligations for WHO-requested verification (Art. 10(2)), non-state source response (Art. 9(2)), and informal consultation (Art. 8) are anchored at actor-only level through DGE NFP designation. No domestic provision specifies 24-hour response windows, internal authorization chains, or WHO communication procedures for these obligations. Anchoring levels: 1 (OBL-007, 008, 009).
ihr2024_implication: IHR 2024 Art. 8 strengthens consultation expectation. Art. 10 adjusts WHO collaboration offer triggers. These changes may increase procedural requirements for the Mexican NFP/National IHR Authority in responding to WHO enquiries and managing consultations. IHR24-CHG-012, IHR24-CHG-013.
pandemic_agreement_implication: Relevant to information and reporting and international cooperation: rapid information sharing and WHO consultation functions will be expected under the Agreement's transparency obligations. PA_ART06_003.
capacity_building_entry_point: Identify whether DGE/DGRI have internal protocols for 24-hour WHO response; consider whether these functions should be codified in an administrative instrument (circular, protocol) to ensure continuity across administrations; review alignment between the strengthened Art. 8 consultation expectation and current SS-DGRI-SRE coordination practice.
notes: Based on OBL-007/008/009. No domestic procedure for Arts. 8-10 verification/consultation identified in corpus. Gap may be filled by administrative practice not captured in the legal corpus. Expert operational review recommended.

current_ihr2005_anchoring_pattern: Partial statutory anchoring. LGS Arts. 181 and 184 provide the statutory basis for emergency health response and resource deployment. Reglamento Interior SS Art. 43 frac. IX assigns territorial emergency response functions. These provisions partially anchor IHR Art. 13 response capacity obligations. IHR Annex 1A response benchmarks are not domestically specified. Anchoring level: 2 (OBL-010, 015).
main_mexico_actors: SS; CSG; DGE; CENAPRECE; entidades federativas; DGRI-SS
main_gap_type: regulatory gap; federal implementation gap; coordination gap
ihr2024_implication: IHR 2024 significantly expands response capacity requirements: pandemic emergency determination activates Art. 13(8) equitable access obligations; Annex 1 national-level capacities now include clinical guidance provision and health product access during response; humanitarian settings are added to response scope. IHR24-CHG-015, 018, 051. The pandemic emergency sub-category may require domestic emergency legislation to distinguish between PHEIC and pandemic emergency response triggers.
pandemic_agreement_implication: Relevant to preparedness and health systems resilience: PA Arts. 6 and 15 require Parties to develop health system functions for equitable emergency response, pandemic preparedness plans, and socioeconomic impact mitigation. PA_ART06_001, PA_ART06_002, PA_ART15_004, PA_ART15_005.
capacity_building_entry_point: Review whether the LGS Art. 181/184 emergency framework distinguishes or needs to distinguish between PHEIC and pandemic emergency response; map federal-state coordination chains for emergency health deployment; assess whether Annex 1A national-level response benchmarks (clinical guidance, health product access) have corresponding domestic implementing instruments.
notes: Based on OBL-010/015. IHR24-CHG-015/018/051. PA_ART06_001-002/PA_ART15_004-005. The reactive emergency powers framework (LGS Arts. 181/184) is present but the proactive capacity-building dimension of IHR Art. 13 is not directly addressed.

current_ihr2005_anchoring_pattern: No specific anchoring identified in available IHR 2005 mapping. IHR 2005 Art. 11 (public communication) and related obligations were not mapped as standalone domestic anchoring obligations in the current corpus. The available corpus does not contain a dedicated risk communication legal framework for public health emergencies. This area requires expanded legal review.
main_mexico_actors: SS; DGE; CSG; CENAPRECE; Secretaría de Gobernación (SEGOB)
ihr2024_implication: IHR 2024 Annex 1 amendments add risk communication and management of health misinformation and disinformation to intermediate-level core capacities. Risk communication is also added to national-level core capacities. IHR24-CHG-050, 051. This may require review of whether Mexico has a dedicated legal or regulatory framework for public health risk communication and infodemic management.
pandemic_agreement_implication: Relevant to prevention and national governance: PA Art. 4 addresses health literacy and community engagement as prevention tools. PA Art. 15(3) requires Parties to promote and facilitate effective and meaningful engagement of relevant stakeholders, including civil society, in pandemic preparedness and response. PA_ART04_012, PA_ART15_003.
capacity_building_entry_point: Identify whether Mexico has a domestic legal or administrative basis for public health risk communication, infodemic management, and coordinated government communication during health emergencies; assess alignment with IHR 2024 Annex 1 intermediate and national-level risk communication capacity requirements.
notes: Risk communication was not mapped as a standalone IHR 2005 obligation in this corpus. Assessment is based on IHR24-CHG-050/051 and PA_ART15_003/PA_ART04_012. Low confidence due to absence from the IHR 2005 mapping baseline. Dedicated legal review required.

current_ihr2005_anchoring_pattern: Partial to moderate regulatory anchoring. The Reglamento LGS Sanidad Internacional (MEX-016) provides the main regulatory framework: Art. 10 designates IHR PoE (airports, ports, ground crossings); Arts. 26-27 specify minimum health service capacities at designated PoE. LGS Art. 352 provides statutory backing. COFEPRIS and SS share competence at PoE. Ship Sanitation Certificate obligations (IHR Arts. 20(2), 36, 39) have weak or no domestic anchoring identified. Anchoring levels: 3 (OBL-016, 019), 2 (OBL-017, 018, 022, 023), 1 (OBL-020, 021).
main_mexico_actors: SS; COFEPRIS; DGE; autoridades portuarias y aeroportuarias; INM; SAT-Aduana (ground crossings)
main_gap_type: partial regulatory gap; procedural gap; update-review needed
ihr2024_implication: IHR 2024 aligns PoE capacity language with the expanded Annex 1 framework and adds laboratory capacity to PHEIC-response PoE capacities (IHR24-CHG-052). PoE core capacity language is updated in Arts. 19-21 (IHR24-CHG-026, 027, 028). These changes may require review of whether Arts. 26-27 of the Reglamento Sanidad Internacional (1985) remain adequate or whether updated regulatory instruments are needed.
pandemic_agreement_implication: Relevant to health systems and preparedness: PA Art. 6 addresses health system functions for equitable emergency response including at subnational and regional levels. PoE are an operational interface for both IHR and Pandemic Agreement implementation. PA_ART06_002.
capacity_building_entry_point: Assess whether Arts. 26-27 of the 1985 Reglamento Sanidad Internacional remain adequate for IHR 2024 expanded Annex 1B PoE capacities; verify which authority is competent to issue Ship Sanitation Certificates and whether this procedure is codified; review ground-crossing health service capacity relative to the designated PoE list in Art. 10.
notes: Based on OBL-016/017/018/019/020/021/022/023. IHR24-CHG-026/027/028/052. The 1985 Reglamento Sanidad Internacional is the principal regulatory anchor for this area and has never been updated for IHR 2005. This creates a systemic currency risk for all PoE-related obligations.

current_ihr2005_anchoring_pattern: Partial statutory and regulatory anchoring. LGS Art. 139 provides statutory authority for health security measures including isolation and quarantine. Reglamento LGS Sanidad Internacional Arts. 68-70 specify isolation/quarantine procedures. Constitutional protections (CPEUM Arts. 1, 14, 29) provide rights-safeguard context. IHR-specific requirements for informed consent, dignity standards, non-discrimination, and traveller information are not operationalised in implementing health-sector provisions. The absence of a charge prohibition (Art. 40) is a full gap. Anchoring levels: 2 (OBL-024 through 031), 0 (OBL-037).
main_mexico_actors: SS; COFEPRIS; DGE; INM; autoridades portuarias y aeroportuarias; CNDH
main_gap_type: rights-safeguard gap; procedural gap; full gap (Art. 40)
ihr2024_implication: IHR 2024 Art. 24 extends conveyance operator health measure obligations to cover embarkation and disembarkation phases (IHR24-CHG-029). Art. 27 explicitly adds quarantine to the list of measures applicable to affected conveyances (IHR24-CHG-030). These extensions may require review of whether Reglamento Sanidad Internacional Art. 44 and related provisions adequately cover the full embarkation-to-disembarkation cycle.
pandemic_agreement_implication: Relevant to health systems and preparedness: PA Art. 6 addresses equitable health system functions including for vulnerable populations; traveller rights safeguards connect to the equity and human rights framing of the Agreement. PA_ART06_001.
capacity_building_entry_point: Review whether implementing health-sector regulations operationalise constitutional rights protections (informed consent, dignity, non-discrimination) in the specific IHR health measure context; assess whether the Art. 40 full gap (no prohibition on traveller charges) requires legislative or regulatory action; examine whether IHR 2024 extensions to embarkation/disembarkation phases are covered by existing provisions.
notes: Based on OBL-024/025/026/027/028/029/030/031/037. IHR24-CHG-029/030. Rights safeguard gaps are consistent across this area and represent a systematic implementation challenge. CNDH advisory competence may be relevant.

current_ihr2005_anchoring_pattern: Partial regulatory anchoring. Reglamento LGS Sanidad Internacional Art. 9 governs health documents at PoE, partially anchoring IHR documentation obligations (Arts. 35, 37, 38, 39). Art. 5 provides aviation-specific definitional anchoring. Specific IHR Maritime Ship Declaration of Health forms, Aircraft General Declaration Health Part procedures, and Ship Sanitation Certificate issuance are not fully codified in the corpus. Anchoring levels: 2 (OBL-032, 034, 035), 1 (OBL-033, 036).
main_mexico_actors: SS; COFEPRIS; SCT (aviation and maritime); autoridades portuarias y aeroportuarias
ihr2024_implication: IHR 2024 Art. 35 explicitly authorises health documents in digital or non-digital format (IHR24-CHG-032). Annex 6 authorises digital vaccination certificates (IHR24-CHG-056). The Maritime Declaration of Health is renamed to Maritime Ship Declaration of Health (IHR24-CHG-033). These changes may require review of whether domestic PoE health documentation requirements accommodate digital formats and whether relevant forms need updating to reflect the renamed instruments.
pandemic_agreement_implication: Relevant to information and reporting: digital health documentation connects to broader health information infrastructure obligations under the Agreement. PA_ART06_003.
capacity_building_entry_point: Assess whether domestic PoE health document requirements (Reglamento Sanidad Internacional Art. 9) need updating for digital formats; verify that the renamed Maritime Ship Declaration of Health and digital vaccination certificate framework are reflected in domestic port health procedures; identify competent authority for issuing and accepting digital health documents at PoE.
notes: Based on OBL-032/033/034/035/036. IHR24-CHG-032/033/056. The 1985 Reglamento Sanidad Internacional predates digital health documentation; currency review is required. SCT (transport authority) may also have relevant regulatory competence not covered by this corpus.

current_ihr2005_anchoring_pattern: Partial to weak anchoring. LGS Arts. 181 and 184 provide authority to implement additional health measures in emergency contexts, partially anchoring IHR Art. 43(1)-(2). The Art. 43(3)/(5) duty to report additional measures to WHO within 48 hours and the Art. 43(6) periodic review obligation are anchored at actor-only level (DGE NFP) with no domestic procedure identified. Anchoring levels: 2 (OBL-040), 1 (OBL-041, 042).
ihr2024_implication: IHR 2024 Art. 43(7) consultation mechanism is expanded to allow requests either directly or through the Director-General, with added confidentiality provisions for consultation information (IHR24-CHG-034). This may require review of whether Mexico has a documented procedure for invoking or responding to Art. 43 consultations, and whether confidentiality obligations for consultation data are addressed domestically.
pandemic_agreement_implication: Relevant to national governance: the articulation of additional health measures with the Agreement's equity and solidarity principles (Art. 3 IHR 2024) may require review of how additional domestic health measures are justified and communicated. PA_ART15_002.
capacity_building_entry_point: Identify whether DGE/DGRI have documented procedures for 48-hour WHO notification of additional health measures; assess whether the expanded Art. 43(7) consultation mechanism requires formalised confidentiality protocols; review whether Art. 43(6) periodic review obligations are operationally tracked.
notes: Based on OBL-040/041/042. IHR24-CHG-034. WHO reporting gaps for additional measures may rely on administrative practice not captured in the legal corpus. Specific review of DGRI/DGE operational protocols recommended.

current_ihr2005_anchoring_pattern: Partial coordination-based anchoring. Reglamento Interior SS Art. 33 assigns DGRI competence for international health cooperation and WHO engagement. LOAPF Art. 28 assigns SRE treaty-management functions. Specific IHR Art. 44(1) collaboration duties (surveillance data sharing, technical support, access to medical countermeasures) are not codified beyond institutional mandates. Anchoring level: 2 (OBL-043).
ihr2024_implication: IHR 2024 significantly expands Art. 44: title now includes financing; preparedness is added to collaboration scope; collaboration to include facilitating access to relevant health products through relevant funding mechanisms. A new coordination function for financing entities is added. IHR24-CHG-035, 037, 038. These changes increase the scope of the collaboration obligation substantially, potentially requiring review of DGRI and SRE mandates.
pandemic_agreement_implication: Relevant to international cooperation and technology transfer: PA Arts. 11 and 12 require Parties to promote technology transfer, licencing, and access to relevant health products through PABS and other mechanisms. PA Art. 13 addresses supply chain and logistics collaboration. PA_ART11_001, PA_ART11_002, PA_ART13_002.
capacity_building_entry_point: Map the current institutional architecture for IHR Art. 44 collaboration functions across DGRI, SRE, and SS; assess whether the expanded IHR 2024 financing dimension of collaboration requires new or updated institutional mandates; review whether Mexico's participation in the Coordinating Financial Mechanism (Art. 44 bis) requires domestic enabling instruments.
notes: Based on OBL-043. IHR24-CHG-035/037/038/039. PA_ART11_001-002/PA_ART13_002. The IHR 2024 financing additions to Art. 44 represent a substantive expansion of collaboration obligations that goes beyond the current DGE/DGRI coordination framework.

current_ihr2005_anchoring_pattern: Indirect contextual anchoring only. LFPRH and Ley de Planeación provide the general budget and planning framework within which IHR capacity investments would be planned and funded. No domestic provision specifically designates sustainable financing for IHR core capacity development, establishes a dedicated health emergency fund, or creates a domestic IHR implementation budget mechanism. Anchoring is contextual (provisions noted as 'Indirect' in mapping).
main_mexico_actors: SS; SHCP; Cámara de Diputados (budget approval); Senado; ASF
ihr2024_implication: IHR 2024 introduces a substantive new financing obligation: States Parties shall provide sustainable domestic funding for IHR implementation (Art. 44, IHR24-CHG-037). A Coordinating Financial Mechanism is established (Art. 44 bis, IHR24-CHG-039). The IHR functioning review must include financing (Art. 54, IHR24-CHG-045). These are new domestic requirements that may require Mexico to identify or designate specific budgetary mechanisms for IHR implementation financing.
pandemic_agreement_implication: Relevant to financing: PA Art. 18 requires Parties to strengthen sustainable and predictable financing for pandemic prevention, preparedness, and response, including through the Coordinating Financial Mechanism. PA_ART18_001, PA_ART18_002, PA_ART18_003.
capacity_building_entry_point: Identify whether existing budgetary instruments (LFPRH, Presupuesto de Egresos) have or require a dedicated IHR implementation budget line or capacity-building programme; assess whether Mexico's participation in the Coordinating Financial Mechanism (IHR Art. 44 bis; PA Art. 18) requires domestic legislative enabling; connect IHR 2024 sustainable financing obligation to the national planning cycle (PND).
notes: Financing is not directly covered as a standalone obligation in the IHR 2005 mapping corpus. Assessment is based on IHR24-CHG-037/039/045 (new IHR 2024 obligations) and PA_ART18_001-003. Low confidence due to absence of specific domestic anchoring in the available corpus. Expert budget law review required.

current_ihr2005_anchoring_pattern: Weak anchoring. IHR reporting obligations to WHO (extension reporting, Art. 5(2); WHO notification obligations generally) are anchored at actor-only level through DGE NFP designation. No domestic provision codifies periodic IHR implementation review or establishes a formal domestic IHR review mechanism. Anchoring levels: 1 (OBL-003, 041, 042).
main_mexico_actors: DGE (NFP); DGRI-SS; SRE; Congreso de la Unión; ASF; Senado
ihr2024_implication: IHR 2024 establishes a States Parties Committee for Implementation (Art. 54 bis, IHR24-CHG-046), which may require Mexico to designate a focal point or reporting mechanism for engagement with the Committee. Art. 54 review now includes financing (IHR24-CHG-045). These changes may increase domestic reporting and review obligations beyond the current NFP-based system.
pandemic_agreement_implication: Relevant to implementation and review: PA Art. 21 establishes periodic reporting obligations to the Conference of the Parties, including on implementation progress and capacity development. PA Art. 31/32 cover ratification and entry into force procedures. PA_ART21_001, PA_ART21_002.
capacity_building_entry_point: Identify whether Mexico has a domestic IHR implementation review mechanism or whether one should be established; designate a focal point for engagement with the IHR 2024 States Parties Committee for Implementation; map the parliamentary reporting chain (Congreso, Senado, ASF) for IHR and Pandemic Agreement implementation oversight; connect to the PA Art. 21 periodic reporting process.
notes: Based on OBL-003/041/042 in IHR 2005 mapping; IHR24-CHG-045/046; PA_ART21_001-002. The States Parties Committee for Implementation is entirely new in IHR 2024 and has no equivalent domestic anchoring in the current corpus.

area: Equitable access to relevant health products
current_ihr2005_anchoring_pattern: No specific anchoring identified in available IHR 2005 mapping corpus. The obligation cluster does not correspond to standalone IHR 2005 obligations in the current mapping. The Reglamento LGS Sanidad Internacional Art. 3 establishes a general WHO/IHR normative hierarchy reference, contextually anchoring WHO recommendations. No domestic provision specifically addresses equitable access to relevant health products during a PHEIC or pandemic emergency. Requires expanded legal review.
ihr2024_implication: IHR 2024 introduces equitable access to relevant health products as a substantive element: Art. 2 adds preparedness and equitable access to the IHR purpose; Art. 13(8) activates State Party collaboration for equitable access upon pandemic emergency determination; Arts. 15 and 16 expand temporary and standing recommendation scope to include relevant health products. A new definition of 'relevant health products' is introduced (IHR24-CHG-003). IHR24-CHG-003, 004, 017, 020, 021, 022, 023. May require review of COFEPRIS regulatory fast-track and emergency procurement frameworks.
pandemic_agreement_implication: Relevant to equitable access, supply chain, technology transfer, and local/regional production: PA Arts. 10, 11, 12, and 13 address equitable geographic distribution of health product manufacturing capacity, technology transfer, licencing, TRIPS flexibilities, and supply chain management. PA_ART10_001, PA_ART11_001, PA_ART13_002.
capacity_building_entry_point: Review COFEPRIS regulatory fast-track and emergency use authorisation frameworks against the expanded 'relevant health products' definition in IHR 2024; assess whether domestic procurement and supply chain legislation is adequate for IHR Art. 13(8) pandemic emergency equitable access obligations; identify technology transfer and local production capacity framework under PA Arts. 10-11; update after PABS final Annex adoption.
notes: This area is not directly represented in the IHR 2005 mapping corpus. Assessment is based on IHR24-CHG-003/004/017/020/021/022/023 (new/expanded IHR 2024 provisions) and PA_ART10_001/PA_ART11_001/PA_ART13_002. Low confidence due to absence of IHR 2005 mapping baseline. PABS dependency is provisional — Based on draft PABS table only; requires update after final annex adoption.

current_ihr2005_anchoring_pattern: Weak anchoring. LGS Art. 146 provides a statutory basis for SS laboratory authority, partially anchoring IHR Annex 1A intermediate-level laboratory confirmation capacity. Reglamento LGS Investigación Art. 11 (international research collaboration including biological samples) provides indirect anchoring for IHR Art. 46 (biological substances). No domestic provision specifically implements IHR Art. 46 import/export facilitation for biological substances and diagnostic reagents. NOM-017 provides governance structure for surveillance laboratories. Anchoring levels: 2 (OBL-013), 1 (OBL-045).
main_mexico_actors: DGE; SS; COFEPRIS; CONAVE; CENAPRECE; InDRE; entidades federativas (state laboratories)
ihr2024_implication: IHR 2024 Annex 1 intermediate-level capacities retain laboratory confirmation as a core requirement. Annex 1B adds laboratory capacity to PHEIC-response PoE capacity requirements (IHR24-CHG-052). These changes may require review of whether PoE laboratory capacity (rapid testing, sample transfer) is adequately specified. Annex 2 middle box expansion to include clusters of severe acute respiratory disease (IHR24-CHG-053) increases the scope of laboratory-confirmed events requiring assessment.
pandemic_agreement_implication: Relevant to PABS: PA Art. 12 establishes a PABS multilateral system covering pathogen sharing and benefit-sharing obligations. PA_ART04_011 requires laboratory biological risk management. PA_ART04_005 requires laboratory-confirmed detection capacity. PA_ART12_006 requires Parties to review and align national/regional pathogen access frameworks with the PABS system. PA_ART12_001, PA_ART12_006.
capacity_building_entry_point: Map the domestic legal framework for laboratory biological risk management, pathogen access, and biosafety/biosecurity against IHR Annex 1A intermediate-level requirements; assess whether a domestic enabling framework for IHR Art. 46 biological substance import/export facilitation exists or is needed; identify PABS-relevant legislation (pathogen access, benefit-sharing, sequence data) for review after final PABS Annex adoption; verify PoE laboratory capacity for PHEIC-response scenarios.
notes: Based on OBL-013/045. IHR24-CHG-052/053. PA_ART04_011/PA_ART12_001/PA_ART12_006. PABS dependency is provisional — Based on draft PABS table only; requires update after final annex adoption. InDRE (Instituto de Diagnóstico y Referencia Epidemiológicos) is the key national laboratory but is not directly named in the current actors file; expert review of laboratory governance should cover InDRE.

current_ihr2005_anchoring_pattern: Relatively stronger statutory anchoring compared to most areas. LGPDPPSO Art. 16 provides an emergency exception for health-related personal data, directly anchoring IHR Art. 45. CPEUM Art. 16 provides constitutional backing. INAI has oversight competence. Coordination between INAI data protection oversight and health-sector data flows in emergencies is partially addressed but not fully operationalised. Anchoring levels: 3 (OBL-044), 2 (contextual constitutional row).
main_mexico_actors: SS; DGE; INAI; CNDH; COFEPRIS; entidades federativas
ihr2024_implication: IHR 2024 Art. 45 processing order clarification (IHR24-CHG-040) does not create new domestic requirements. The broader expansion of digital health documentation (Arts. 35, 44; Annex 6) increases the volume of personal health data flowing through PoE and health documentation systems, which may require review of data governance frameworks for digital health records in public health emergency contexts.
pandemic_agreement_implication: Relevant to information and reporting and national governance: PA Art. 6(3) requires national health information systems including data sharing mechanisms; PA Art. 15 addresses transparency and accountability. Data protection and information governance are cross-cutting obligations. PA_ART06_003.
capacity_building_entry_point: Review operational coordination protocols between INAI oversight functions and SS/DGE health surveillance data flows; assess whether the LGPDPPSO Art. 16 emergency exception framework is operationally aligned with IHR Art. 45 requirements including data minimisation, confidentiality, and handling rules; examine whether digital health documentation (IHR 2024) creates new personal data governance obligations.
notes: Based on OBL-044. IHR24-CHG-040. PA_ART06_003. This is one of the few areas with level-3 anchoring in the IHR 2005 mapping. The main gap is operational coordination between the data protection and health surveillance legal frameworks, not the absence of a legal basis.

current_ihr2005_anchoring_pattern: Partial statutory anchoring with federal implementation gap. LGS Art. 13 distributes health competences between federal and state levels; Art. 9o Bis establishes the Sistema Federal de Salud (SFSS) coordination framework. SINAVE governance (NOM-017) covers federal-state surveillance coordination. However, the IHR 2005 mapping does not identify specific provisions that operationalise federal-to-state translation of IHR obligations, time-bound implementation chains, or federal-state emergency coordination protocols. Anchoring level: 2 (various OBLs).
main_mexico_actors: SS; entidades federativas (32); CSG; CENAPRECE; DGE; CONAVE
main_gap_type: federal implementation gap; coordination gap; procedural gap
ihr2024_implication: IHR 2024 Annex 1 expansions increase the scope of local-level and intermediate-level core capacities, directly implicating state and municipal health systems. IHR24-CHG-049 (local level: health services access, community engagement) and IHR24-CHG-050 (intermediate level: coordination, risk communication) are obligations that require operational translation to sub-national levels. This may require review of federal-state coordination instruments for IHR implementation.
pandemic_agreement_implication: Relevant to health systems and national governance: PA Art. 6 addresses national and, where appropriate, regional and subnational health system functions; PA Art. 7(3) asks Parties to designate national, subnational, and local health workforce focal points. PA_ART06_001, PA_ART07_003.
capacity_building_entry_point: Map how LGS Art. 13 federal-state competence distribution is operationalised for IHR implementation; assess whether there are administrative agreements (convenios de coordinación) between federal SS and state health secretariats for IHR-specific obligations; review whether IHR 2024 expanded local and intermediate Annex 1 capacities require new or updated federal-state coordination instruments.
notes: Based on federal distribution provisions in OBL-002/012/013/015 mapping rows and general LGS Art. 13 anchoring pattern. State-level legislation and federal-state convenios are not included in this corpus, representing a significant coverage gap for federal implementation assessment.

current_ihr2005_anchoring_pattern: Indirect contextual anchoring. The Senado approves treaties under CPEUM Art. 76 frac. I (applicable to IHR and Pandemic Agreement). The Cámara de Diputados approves the health sector budget (applicable to IHR implementation resources). ASF audits health sector expenditure. CNDH has advisory and investigatory functions for rights compliance. None of these functions are specifically configured for IHR or Pandemic Agreement oversight. No dedicated parliamentary review mechanism for international health obligations exists in the available corpus.
main_mexico_actors: Senado de la República; Cámara de Diputados; ASF; CNDH; INAI
ihr2024_implication: IHR 2024 Art. 54 bis establishes a States Parties Committee for Implementation, which will require State Party engagement and domestic reporting linkages. Art. 54(2) requires Health Assembly review to include financing (IHR24-CHG-045). These new review mechanisms may require Mexico to designate a parliamentary or executive reporting channel for IHR Committee interactions. IHR24-CHG-046.
pandemic_agreement_implication: Relevant to implementation and review: PA Art. 21 establishes periodic reporting to the Conference of the Parties. PA Art. 31/32 require ratification through national constitutional procedures (Senado). The Pandemic Agreement will require parliamentary engagement at ratification and potentially for implementing legislation. PA_ART21_001, PA_ART31_001, PA_ART32_001.
capacity_building_entry_point: Identify whether the Senado or relevant Senate committee has a standing review function for IHR and Pandemic Agreement implementation; assess whether ASF audit scope covers IHR core capacity spending as a distinct category; prepare a legislative mapping of the implementing legislation required before Pandemic Agreement ratification; designate an engagement point for the IHR 2024 States Parties Committee for Implementation.
notes: Based on general actor anchoring from mexico_health_governance_actors.csv. IHR24-CHG-046. PA_ART21_001/PA_ART31_001/PA_ART32_001. Parliamentary oversight architecture for international health instruments is not directly addressed in the IHR 2005 mapping and requires dedicated political and constitutional review.

current_ihr2005_anchoring_pattern: No specific anchoring identified in available IHR 2005 mapping. IHR Art. 46 (biological substances) is the closest IHR 2005 provision, with weak anchoring (level 1 only, RLGS Investigación Art. 11). No domestic framework for pathogen access and benefit-sharing has been identified in the available corpus. This area requires expanded legal review and will be substantially defined by the final PABS Annex text.
main_mexico_actors: SS; COFEPRIS; DGE; InDRE; SRE; SHCP; national laboratory network
ihr2024_implication: No direct IHR 2024 PABS provision (PABS is an instrument of the Pandemic Agreement, not IHR 2024). Indirectly: IHR 2024 'relevant health products' definition (Art. 1) and equitable access provisions (Art. 13(8)) are operationally linked to PABS benefit-sharing obligations under PA Art. 12.
pandemic_agreement_implication: Directly relevant: PA Art. 12 establishes the multilateral PABS system. PA Art. 12(6) requires Parties to review and, as appropriate, align national/regional pathogen access frameworks with the PABS system. Draft PABS Annex covers: scope and objectives; PABS Materials and Sequence Information; access obligations; benefit-sharing; governance. PA_ART12_001, PA_ART12_006.
capacity_building_entry_point: Review whether Mexico has domestic legislation governing pathogen sample collection, storage, and international transfer that would need alignment with PABS system rules; identify whether COFEPRIS/SENASICA/InDRE roles in pathogen access and benefit-sharing are clearly defined; prepare a legislative review matrix for PABS Annex implementation after final adoption; assess TRIPS flexibilities and technology transfer provisions (PA Arts. 11-12) for relevance to domestic law. Note: Based on draft PABS table only; requires update after final annex adoption.
notes: Based on OBL-045 (level 1 only). Draft PABS Annex (IGWG Bureau draft, 2026-03-09). PA_ART12_001/PA_ART12_006.
